parentQuestion: Osmosis is the movement of ___ across a semipermeable membrane.

options: Movement of water across a semipermeable membrane
scaffolds.explain: OSMOSIS is the movement of WATER across a SEMIPERMEABLE MEMBRANE from low solute to high solute concentration.

wrongExplains.0: Osmosis specifically refers to water movement.
wrongExplains.2: Active transport uses ATP; osmosis is passive water movement.
wrongExplains.3: Proteins are too large; osmosis moves water.

scaffolds.explain: A SOLUTE is the substance BEING DISSOLVED (like salt in water). The solute dissolves in the solvent.

wrongExplains.0: The solvent dissolves; the solute is dissolved.
wrongExplains.2: Pure water is a solvent; solutes dissolve in it.
wrongExplains.3: Membranes are structures; solutes are dissolved substances.

options: The substance doing the dissolving (like water)
scaffolds.explain: A SOLVENT is the substance DOING THE DISSOLVING. Water is the most common solvent in biology — the 'universal solvent.'

wrongExplains.0: The solute is dissolved; the solvent does the dissolving.
wrongExplains.2: Salt is a solute; water is the solvent.
wrongExplains.3: Proteins are solutes; water is typically the solvent.

options: A membrane that allows some substances through but not others
A membrane that lets everything through
scaffolds.explain: A SEMIPERMEABLE MEMBRANE allows SOME SUBSTANCES through but NOT OTHERS. Cell membranes are semipermeable.

wrongExplains.2: Letting everything through would be fully permeable.
wrongExplains.3: Membranes are selective barriers, not solid walls.

scaffolds.q: In which direction does water move during osmosis?
options: From high solute to low solute
From low solute (high water) to high solute (low water)
scaffolds.explain: Water moves from LOW SOLUTE (high water) to HIGH SOLUTE (low water). Water follows the gradient to dilute the solute.

wrongExplains.0: Water moves toward high solute, not away from it.
wrongExplains.2: Osmosis has a specific direction — toward higher solute.
wrongExplains.3: Water can move into or out of cells depending on conditions.

options: Higher solute concentration than the cell
scaffolds.explain: A HYPERTONIC solution has HIGHER SOLUTE concentration than the cell. Water leaves cells in hypertonic solutions.

wrongExplains.0: Lower solute is hypotonic; hypertonic has more solute.
wrongExplains.2: Equal concentration is isotonic; hypertonic has more.
wrongExplains.3: Pure water is hypotonic; hypertonic has high solute.

options: Lower solute concentration than the cell
scaffolds.explain: A HYPOTONIC solution has LOWER SOLUTE concentration than the cell. Water enters cells in hypotonic solutions.

wrongExplains.0: Higher solute is hypertonic; hypotonic has less.
wrongExplains.2: Equal concentration is isotonic; hypotonic has less.
wrongExplains.3: Concentrated salt is hypertonic; hypotonic has low solute.

scaffolds.q: What happens to a cell in a hypotonic solution?
scaffolds.explain: In a HYPOTONIC solution, water enters the cell causing it to SWELL and possibly BURST (lyse). The cell has higher solute.

wrongExplains.2: Osmosis causes water movement and cell volume changes.
wrongExplains.3: Division is mitosis; hypotonic solutions cause swelling.

scaffolds.q: Osmosis is the movement of ___ across a semipermeable membrane?
scaffolds.explain: Osmosis is the movement of WATER across a semipermeable membrane from low solute to high solute concentration.

wrongExplains.0: Solute movement is diffusion; osmosis specifically moves water.
wrongExplains.2: Proteins are too large; osmosis moves water.
wrongExplains.3: Ion movement requires channels or pumps; osmosis moves water.
